title: Walnut and raisin loaf
subtitle: A brown yeasted loaf with toasted walnuts and raisins
excerpt: A slightly enriched brown loaf studded with toasted walnut pieces and raisins. Best with cheese and chutney, or sliced thick and spread with cold butter. The walnuts are toasted before going into the dough to deepen their flavour.
sourceNotes: Nut and dried-fruit breads are documented in British and Continental baking literature from the mid-nineteenth century. Mrs Beeton records a raisin bread in the 1861 edition of the Book of Household Management (Project Gutenberg #10136). Walnut bread is a French tradition particularly associated with the Périgord region, and crossed into British baking in the early twentieth century through artisan bakeries. The combination of walnuts and raisins in a brown loaf follows the classic British artisan approach.

content.text: Toast the walnuts first. Untoasted walnuts in bread are mildly bitter; five minutes in a 180°C oven or a dry frying pan brings out their oil and sweetness. Let them cool before adding to the dough or they will affect the yeast activity.

Combine both flours, yeast, and salt. Dissolve the honey in the lukewarm water and add to the flour. Mix to a rough dough, then knead for 10 minutes until smooth and elastic. Add the cooled walnuts and raisins and knead for a further 2 minutes to distribute them. Cover and leave at room temperature for 1 hour until doubled.
the dough and shaping it into a log that fits the loaf tin. Place seam-side down in the greased tin. Cover and prove for 35 to 45 minutes until the dough has risen about 2 cm above the rim.

Preheat the oven to 220°C conventional with a water tray on the floor. Bake for 10 minutes, then reduce to 200°C and bake for a further 20 to 25 minutes. The loaf is done when the top is deep brown, the sides have pulled away from the tin, and an internal probe reads 96°C. Cool on a rack for at least 1 hour before slicing. Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more; use an oven thermometer for consistent results.
